story:
At dawn, guards pulled Henryk from death row and marched him past places inmates never saw. He wasn’t going to the chair. He was going somewhere worse. Three others joined him: Julianna, shaking and insisting she’d been framed for her sister’s crime; Todd, whose stare crawled under the skin; and Richard—Henryk’s father—locked up for killing a judge and destroying evidence in a doomed attempt to save his son after Henryk’s own deadly rampage.

They were led to a vast concrete room where a lone wooden door stood in a frame with no wall behind it. The Chief held up a key the color of deep water, a blue that seemed to glow from its core. SCP-860, he said—a key that opens any door, not to where it should lead, but to somewhere else. Guns made refusal impossible.

Richard took the key. When it turned in the lock, it made a sound like bones breaking underwater. The door swung open to reveal a blue-tinted forest—trees with pulsing bark, a dirt path that split and branched like veins, air thick with copper and ozone, silence as heavy as a burial shroud. A cable-thin retrieval line, clipped to the key’s bow before they entered, hissed out after them. The door clicked shut behind them.

A low, wrong purring threaded the quiet, growing louder as they followed the path because there was nothing else to do. Fear made people talk. Todd admitted he’d hurt a girl. Henryk snarled at him to shut up. Richard confessed he’d been a cybersecurity specialist who had thrown away his life to protect his son. Henryk, hollow with guilt, asked why. “You’re my son,” Richard said. It didn’t erase the harm, but it explained the ruin.

The purr cracked into a roar. “Hide!” Henryk shouted. They bolted into the blue undergrowth. Todd broke away first, crashing downslope through the brush. Julianna stumbled, tumbled down an embankment, and snapped her leg. She rolled into a cave glowing with mist and blue crystals and found what was left of Todd—torn apart, scattered like a toy taken to pieces. She gagged, then dragged herself out, her blood a dark smear on the moss.

Above, Henryk and Richard found one another, the path gone, the forest endless and tilted wrong. “We’re going to die,” Henryk said. He thought maybe he deserved to. Richard pulled him close and, for a breath, they were only father and son again—remembering how little Henryk used to go down every slide headfirst because waterslides made everything look the same. Stubborn then. Doomed now.

Julianna burst through the brush, bright with a last spark. “A door! A white door with a keyhole!” Hope lifted its head—and the forest exploded.

It came like a nightmare given bones: cat-shaped, truck-sized, made of porcelain, wood, and living vines, eyes like holes that fell forever, a mouth with too many teeth. It took Julianna in one bite, shook her, and flung her body into Richard, pinning him. “Run,” Richard mouthed as the teeth closed. Henryk didn’t. Kneeling in the blue moss, he finally understood. “I deserve this,” he said to the thing. It tilted its head, curious, and lunged.

Back in containment, the Chief listened without blinking. All four D-Class: deceased. Unknown entity within SCP-860-1. Insufficient data. An orderly reeled in the retrieval line; at its end, the blue key knocked softly against the sealed doorframe. He ordered the next group. No warnings. They were death row, he said. Their lives were already forfeit.
